# Python Enterprise CRUD Demo

This Quarto book walks through a small but realistic enterprise application for third-semester Management Information Systems students. The example focuses on a B2B discount maintenance scenario and demonstrates how Python applications are typically structured when they read from and write to a Microsoft SQL Server database.

::: {.overview-grid}
::: {.overview-item}
::: {.overview-label}
Stack
:::
Python 3.13, `uv`, Streamlit, `pandas`, `sqlalchemy`, `pyodbc`, and Quarto.
:::

::: {.overview-item}
::: {.overview-label}
Database
:::
Read, insert, and update operations are routed through role-based SQL Server views instead of the base table.
:::

::: {.overview-item}
::: {.overview-label}
Architecture
:::
The codebase is separated into presentation, business logic, and data access so each layer has one clear responsibility.
:::
:::

## Learning Goals

By the end of this project, students should be able to:

- set up a Python 3.13 project with `uv`
- connect to Microsoft SQL Server with `pyodbc`, `sqlalchemy`, and `pandas`
- separate presentation, business logic, and data access into clear layers
- implement typical CRUD-style flows in a Streamlit user interface
- handle database errors and validation messages in a user-friendly way

## Business Scenario

The application manages quantity-based B2B discounts. Sales staff need an internal tool to:

- view existing discount rules
- add a new discount for a selected customer
- edit an existing discount if business conditions change

The SQL Server already exposes role-based views for this workflow, so the Python application does not work directly on the base table. This mirrors a common enterprise setup where database permissions are restricted through views.

## Architecture Overview

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    U["User in Browser"] --> A["Presentation Layer<br>app.py<br>Streamlit tabs"]
    A --> B["Business Logic Layer<br>services/discount_service.py<br>validation and rules"]
    B --> C["Data Access Layer<br>repositories/db.py<br>repositories/discount_repo.py"]
    C --> D["Role-Based Views<br>list_views / ins_views / upd_views<br>dbo.LOV_CUSTOMER"]
    D --> E["SQL Server Database<br>ERPDEV"]
```

## Database Objects Used in the Demo

| Purpose | Object |
| --- | --- |
| Read discount overview | `list_views.V_LIST_B2B_DISCOUNT` |
| Insert new discount | `ins_views.V_INS_B2B_DISCOUNT` |
| Update existing discount | `upd_views.V_UPD_B2B_DISCOUNT` |
| Fill customer dropdown | `dbo.LOV_CUSTOMER` |

## Project Structure

```text
.
├── app.py
├── models/discount.py
├── repositories/db.py
├── repositories/discount_repo.py
├── services/discount_service.py
├── sql_example.py
└── README.md
```

## What Students Should Notice

- `app.py` contains no raw SQL. The presentation layer only deals with user interaction.
- `discount_service.py` contains no Streamlit code. It focuses on validation and business meaning.
- `discount_repo.py` is the only place where SQL statements are written.
- `.env` keeps credentials out of the source code.

:::{.callout-tip}
This separation is not just a style preference. It makes the application easier to test, easier to explain in class, and easier to extend later.
:::

## Suggested Workflow

1. Complete the operating-system-specific setup steps.
2. Configure your `.env` file.
3. Test the MSSQL connection with `sql_example.py`.
4. Start the Streamlit application with `uv run streamlit run app.py`.
5. Inspect how each UI action maps to the business and data layers.
